title: Recording Japan's disabled lives
gist: Ten years after the Sagamihara killings, Japan's attitudes to disability are slowly shifting: courts have struck down its eugenics-era sterilisation law and disability employment quotas are rising, even as the death penalty and a 99% conviction rate persist.

insights.claim: In 2016 Satoshi Uematsu murdered 19 and injured 26 at the Tsukui Yamayuri-en disability facility in Sagamihara - the worst single-perpetrator act of violence against disabled people anywhere in modern history.
insights.so_what: Baseline event shaping Japan's contemporary disability debate.
insights.speaker: Christopher Harding

insights.claim: Japan's postwar Eugenic Protection Law (1948) permitted voluntary and involuntary sterilisation; around 25,000 people were sterilised (16,500 non-consensually) and it remained in force until 1996.
insights.so_what: Scale of a state eugenics program that persisted into the late 20th century.
insights.speaker: Christopher Harding

insights.claim: In 2024 Japan's Supreme Court ruled the Eugenic Protection Law unconstitutional and affirmed state responsibility, leading to ordered compensation of 15 million yen (about GBP 80,000) per survivor.
insights.so_what: Landmark accountability ruling reversing decades of state non-liability.
insights.speaker: Christopher Harding

insights.claim: Japan's criminal conviction rate exceeds 99 per cent, largely because prosecutors only pursue cases where the evidence makes conviction nearly inevitable.
insights.so_what: Structural feature of Japanese justice with implications for wrongful-conviction risk.
insights.speaker: Christopher Harding

insights.claim: Iwao Hakamata was acquitted at retrial after 46 years on death row, with investigators found to have tampered with evidence to secure his 1966 conviction.
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence that even a 99% system produces catastrophic wrongful convictions.
insights.speaker: Christopher Harding

insights.claim: Japan executes by long-drop hanging with the condemned often notified less than an hour beforehand and families told only afterward, as executions happen in secret.
insights.so_what: Details underpinning international criticism of Japan's capital punishment regime.
insights.speaker: Christopher Harding

insights.claim: Japan recently raised its mandatory disability employment rate for larger companies from 2.5 to 2.7 per cent, with a 3 per cent requirement for national and local government.
insights.so_what: Regulatory pressure on employers to integrate disabled workers, though quotas are hard to meet.
insights.speaker: Christopher Harding

insights.claim: Stigma is slowly ebbing - names are being added to the Sagamihara memorial cenotaph after families initially requested anonymity for murdered relatives, citing Japan's 'deep-rooted eugenic outlook'.
insights.so_what: Marker of gradual cultural change in how disability is publicly regarded in Japan.
insights.speaker: Christopher Harding
